title: Ecological niches
summary: The ecological niche is the full role a species plays in its community — how it obtains energy and nutrients, the conditions it tolerates, and how it interacts with every other species around it. This lesson builds the whole of B4.2: the niche concept and its resource and interaction dimensions; the difference between the fundamental and realised niche; the modes of nutrition that define how organisms feed (autotrophs and photoautotrophs, the heterotroph types — consumers, saprotrophs, detritivores — plus mixotrophs, and holozoic, parasitic and saprotrophic nutrition); competition, both intraspecific and interspecific; the competitive exclusion principle; how resource partitioning lets similar species coexist; and how all of this shapes the structure of a community. The explanation worked examples are laid out exactly the way our marking engine awards Paper 2 marks — one mark per distinct valid point, with ECF — so you can see how the way you phrase an answer decides how many marks you keep.
learningObjectives: State the niche concept — the role of a species including its use of resources and its interactions — and distinguish the niche from the habitat.
Distinguish between the fundamental niche and the realised niche, and explain why the realised niche is usually the smaller of the two.

Classify organisms by mode of nutrition: autotrophs (photoautotrophs) and heterotrophs (consumers, saprotrophs/decomposers, detritivores), including mixotrophs, and describe holozoic, parasitic and saprotrophic nutrition.
Distinguish intraspecific from interspecific competition and apply the competitive exclusion principle.
Explain how resource partitioning reduces competition and allows similar species to coexist, and how a species' niche contributes to community structure.
Present explanation answers as distinct, creditable points so that analytic marking and error-carried-forward protect your score.
levelNote: Common to SL and HL at this level.

simpleExplanation.title: An organism's job, not just its address
simpleExplanation.summary: A habitat is where an organism lives; a niche is everything it does. The niche bundles together how a species makes its living — what it eats and how it feeds, the conditions it can tolerate, and every interaction it has with other species. Because two species cannot make an identical living side by side forever, the niche concept explains both why species compete and how they manage to share a place.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of a busy high street. A shop's habitat is its postcode — the physical unit it occupies. Its niche is its whole business model: what it sells, who its suppliers and customers are, its opening hours, and which shops it competes with. Two cafés on the same street with the exact same menu, prices and hours would fight over every customer until one closed — that is competitive exclusion. In practice they survive by differing: one opens early for commuters and sells coffee, the other opens late and sells cocktails. Splitting the trade like that is resource partitioning, and it is why several similar businesses can share one street.
steps: Separate the habitat (the physical 'address', set by abiotic factors such as temperature and pH) from the niche (the functional 'job', which also includes every biotic interaction).
Describe how the species obtains its energy and nutrients — its mode of nutrition — because feeding is the core of any niche.
Distinguish the fundamental niche (everything the species could do in the absence of competitors) from the realised niche (what it actually does once competitors are present).

Work out what happens when niches overlap: either the stronger competitor excludes the other (competitive exclusion) or the species divide the resource (resource partitioning) and coexist, which in turn shapes the whole community.

flashcards.back: The role a species plays in its community — its use of resources (food, space, light, nutrients) together with all its interactions, both biotic (competition, predation, disease) and abiotic (temperature, pH, humidity). Not just where it lives, but everything it does.

The HABITAT is the physical place a species lives (its 'address'). The NICHE includes the habitat but adds the species' functional role — how it feeds and every interaction it has. Habitat is one dimension of the niche, not a synonym for it.

The full range of conditions and resources under which a species could survive and reproduce in the ABSENCE of interspecific competition (and other limiting biotic interactions). The species' potential niche.

The actual range of conditions and resources a species occupies once competitors and other species are present. It is a subset of, or at most equal to, the fundamental niche — usually smaller because competition restricts it.

An organism that synthesises its own organic (carbon) compounds from inorganic sources such as CO₂. A PHOTOautotroph uses light energy for this — e.g. plants, algae and cyanobacteria. Autotrophs are the producers of a community.

An organism that obtains organic carbon compounds by taking in and using ready-made organic matter from other organisms. Includes consumers, saprotrophs and detritivores — none can make their own food.

An organism that can use BOTH autotrophic and heterotrophic nutrition — e.g. Euglena, which photosynthesises in light but ingests food particles when light is scarce. Facultative mixotrophs switch as conditions demand.
The mode of most animals (consumers): internal digestion. Food is INGESTED, then digested inside the body, absorbed, and the waste egested. Contrast with saprotrophs, which digest externally.

A heterotroph (mainly fungi and bacteria) that feeds on DEAD or decaying organic matter by secreting digestive enzymes onto it and absorbing the products — external digestion. Saprotrophs are the decomposers that recycle nutrients.

A heterotroph that ingests non-living organic matter (detritus) and digests it INTERNALLY — e.g. earthworms, woodlice. Like saprotrophs it feeds on dead matter, but by ingestion (holozoic) not external digestion.

A heterotroph (the parasite) obtains nutrients from a living host, harming it. May be ecto- (on the host, e.g. ticks) or endoparasitic (inside, e.g. tapeworms). The host is damaged but usually not immediately killed.

INTRAspecific = between members of the SAME species (identical niches, so the most intense competition). INTERspecific = between members of DIFFERENT species whose niches overlap. Only interspecific competition drives competitive exclusion.

Two species cannot occupy the exact same niche in the same place indefinitely: the more effective competitor for the shared limiting resource eventually excludes the other locally. 'Complete competitors cannot coexist.'
Similar species divide a shared resource — using it in different places, at different times, or in different forms — so their realised niches differ. This reduces competition and allows coexistence.

Both feed on DEAD matter, so the mark is in the MECHANISM. Saprotrophs digest EXTERNALLY (secrete enzymes, absorb products); detritivores INGEST detritus and digest it INTERNALLY. Don't just say 'they eat dead things'.

The fundamental niche is the BIGGER, potential one (no competitors); the realised niche is the SMALLER, actual one (competitors present). Removing a competitor lets the realised niche EXPAND toward the fundamental — not the other way round.

quickCheck.prompt: A competitor species is experimentally removed and the remaining species spreads into areas it never used before. The area it now occupies is best described as its:
options: Fundamental niche, revealed once competition was removed
A new niche it has just evolved
quickCheck.answer: Fundamental niche, revealed once competition was removed

quickCheck.prompt: Which pair of feeding modes are BOTH heterotrophic but differ in WHERE digestion happens?
According to the competitive exclusion principle, two species occupying the identical niche in the same place will:
options: Coexist indefinitely by sharing resources equally
Not coexist indefinitely — the better competitor excludes the other locally
Interbreed to form a single species
quickCheck.answer: Not coexist indefinitely — the better competitor excludes the other locally

quickCheck.prompt: Euglena photosynthesises in bright light but ingests organic particles in the dark. Its mode of nutrition is best described as:
Two warbler species feed on insects in the SAME trees but at different heights and on different branches, so both persist. This is an example of:

faq.q: Is a niche the same as a habitat?
faq.a: No, and examiners test this directly. A habitat is the physical place a species lives — its 'address', defined mainly by abiotic conditions. A niche is the full role the species plays: its habitat PLUS how it obtains energy and nutrients (its mode of nutrition), the conditions it tolerates, and every interaction it has with other species. The habitat is just one dimension of the niche.

faq.q: Why is the realised niche usually smaller than the fundamental niche?
faq.a: The fundamental niche is what a species could do with no competitors present. In a real community, other species compete for the shared resources, so the species is restricted to the part of its potential niche where it can hold its own — the realised niche. Remove the competitor and the realised niche expands back toward the fundamental niche, which is exactly how ecologists demonstrate the difference experimentally.

faq.q: What is the difference between a saprotroph and a detritivore, since both feed on dead matter?
faq.a: The distinction is the site of digestion. A saprotroph (mostly fungi and bacteria) secretes digestive enzymes ONTO the dead organic matter and absorbs the soluble products — digestion is external, and this is saprotrophic nutrition. A detritivore (e.g. an earthworm or woodlouse) INGESTS the dead matter and digests it internally, which is holozoic. Both are decomposers in effect and both recycle nutrients, but the mechanism is what earns the mark.

faq.q: Does intraspecific or interspecific competition cause competitive exclusion?
faq.a: Competitive exclusion applies to INTERspecific competition — two different species with overlapping niches. Intraspecific competition is between members of the same species, whose niches are identical by definition, so it is actually the most intense form of competition and a strong driver of natural selection; but it cannot 'exclude' the species from itself. Only interspecific competition can drive one species out in favour of another.

faq.q: How does the niche concept explain the structure of a whole community?
faq.a: Because competitive exclusion prevents identical niches from coexisting, the species that do live together must each occupy a distinct realised niche — they partition the resources. The result is a community in which species are spread across different feeding modes, food sources, microhabitats and activity times. Niche differences therefore set how many species a community can hold and how they are organised, from producers through the various heterotroph roles.

sections.content: Why can a dozen insect-eating bird species share a single forest, while two species of the same barnacle cannot share one patch of rock? The answer is the ecological niche — the complete role a species plays in its community. A niche is far more than an address on a map: it bundles together how a species feeds, the conditions it tolerates, and every interaction it has with the organisms around it. This lesson builds the niche concept from the ground up: the fundamental and realised niche, the modes of nutrition that define how organisms make their living, competition and the competitive exclusion principle, and how resource partitioning lets similar species share a place and so shapes the structure of the whole community. The explanation examples are marked exactly the way our engine marks Paper 2 — one mark per distinct valid idea — so you can see how the way you word an answer decides your score.
The niche concept: role, resources and interactions

The ecological niche of a species is the role it plays in its community — the way it uses resources together with the way it interacts with everything else. It is easy to confuse the niche with the habitat, but they are not the same. The habitat is the physical place a species lives, its 'address', and it is largely a matter of abiotic conditions such as temperature, pH, salinity and light. The niche is the species' 'profession': it includes the habitat, but it also captures how the species obtains energy and nutrients, when and where it is active, the range of abiotic conditions it can tolerate, and its biotic interactions — what it eats, what eats it, what it competes with and what it depends on. Two species can share a habitat while occupying quite different niches; what they cannot do is share an identical niche.

items: **Niche:** the role of a species in its community — its use of resources AND its interactions.
**Habitat:** the physical place a species lives; one abiotic dimension of the niche, not the whole niche.
A niche has **abiotic dimensions** (temperature, pH, humidity, light — the conditions tolerated) and **biotic dimensions** (feeding, competition, predation, disease).
Mode of nutrition — how the species obtains energy and carbon — sits at the core of every niche.

sections.content: Modes of nutrition

Because feeding is the heart of a niche, organisms are first classified by how they obtain organic carbon compounds and energy. AUTOTROPHS synthesise their own organic compounds from inorganic raw materials such as carbon dioxide; a PHOTOautotroph does this using light energy, and plants, algae and cyanobacteria are the photoautotrophs that act as the producers of most communities. HETEROTROPHS cannot make their own organic compounds and must obtain them ready-made from other organisms. A few organisms are MIXOTROPHS, able to use both strategies — Euglena, for example, photosynthesises in the light but ingests organic particles when light is scarce; facultative mixotrophs switch between the modes as conditions change.

Heterotrophs are subdivided by what they feed on and how. CONSUMERS feed on other living organisms and typically show HOLOZOIC nutrition: food is ingested, digested internally, absorbed, and the indigestible remainder egested — the pattern in most animals. SAPROTROPHS (mainly fungi and bacteria) feed on dead or decaying organic matter by SAPROTROPHIC nutrition: they secrete digestive enzymes onto the material and absorb the soluble products, so digestion is external — these are the decomposers that recycle nutrients back to producers. DETRITIVORES, such as earthworms and woodlice, also feed on dead organic matter (detritus) but INGEST it and digest it internally, so they are holozoic feeders that happen to eat detritus. Finally, in PARASITIC nutrition a heterotroph obtains nutrients from a living host, harming it — parasites may live on the host (ectoparasites such as ticks) or inside it (endoparasites such as tapeworms).

items: **Autotroph (photoautotroph):** makes its own organic compounds from CO₂, using light energy — the producers (plants, algae, cyanobacteria).
**Heterotroph:** obtains ready-made organic compounds from other organisms.
**Consumer (holozoic):** ingests food, digests it INTERNALLY, absorbs, egests waste.
**Saprotroph (saprotrophic):** feeds on DEAD matter by EXTERNAL digestion — secretes enzymes, absorbs products; a decomposer.
**Detritivore:** ingests DEAD organic matter and digests it INTERNALLY (holozoic).
**Parasitic:** obtains nutrients from a LIVING host, harming it (ecto- or endoparasite).
**Mixotroph:** can use BOTH autotrophic and heterotrophic nutrition (e.g. Euglena).

sections.content: When a question asks you to distinguish saprotrophs from detritivores, the marks are for the MECHANISM, not the food. Both feed on dead matter, so state the difference: saprotrophs digest EXTERNALLY (enzymes secreted, soluble products absorbed) while detritivores INGEST detritus and digest it INTERNALLY. Answers that only say 'both eat dead things' score nothing on the distinguishing point.
Fundamental and realised niche

No species lives in isolation, and the presence of competitors changes what part of its potential niche a species can actually occupy. The FUNDAMENTAL niche is the full range of conditions and resources under which a species could survive and reproduce in the ABSENCE of interspecific competition — its potential, defined by its own tolerances and requirements. The REALISED niche is the part of that range the species actually occupies once competitors (and other limiting species) are present. Because competition tends to restrict a species to where it competes best, the realised niche is a subset of, or at most equal to, the fundamental niche — in practice, usually smaller. The clearest way to demonstrate the difference is experimental: remove the competitor, and the species expands to fill more of its fundamental niche.

sections.question: On a rocky shore, two barnacle species occupy different zones. The small barnacle *Chthamalus* is found only in the upper shore, while the larger *Balanus* dominates the middle and lower shore. When researchers experimentally removed *Balanus* from the middle and lower zones, *Chthamalus* larvae settled and grew successfully throughout the whole shore. Using this information: (a) state the realised niche of *Chthamalus*; (b) deduce its fundamental niche and explain your reasoning. [3]
sections.solution:
**(a) Realised niche of *Chthamalus*** [1 mark]
The realised niche of *Chthamalus* is the UPPER shore only — that is where it is actually found when *Balanus* is present. [1]

**(b) Fundamental niche and reasoning** [2 marks]
The fundamental niche of *Chthamalus* is the WHOLE shore — upper, middle and lower zones. [1] We can deduce this because, once the competitor *Balanus* was removed, *Chthamalus* survived and grew across all zones; it is therefore physiologically able to occupy the whole shore, and is normally excluded from the middle and lower zones by interspecific competition with *Balanus*. [1]

*Note the direction of the logic: removing a competitor lets the realised niche EXPAND toward the fundamental niche. A common error is to describe it the other way round.*

sections.content: Competition: intraspecific and interspecific

Whenever a resource is limited and more than one organism needs it, there is competition. INTRAspecific competition is between members of the SAME species. Because individuals of one species have essentially identical niches, they demand exactly the same resources, so intraspecific competition is the most intense form and a powerful agent of natural selection. INTERspecific competition is between members of DIFFERENT species whose niches overlap; the more the niches overlap, the more the two species compete. It is interspecific competition — not intraspecific — that can lead to one species being driven out, and it is what restricts a species from its fundamental to its realised niche.

items: **Intraspecific competition:** same species, identical niches — the most intense competition; drives natural selection.
**Interspecific competition:** different species with overlapping niches; intensity increases with the degree of overlap.
Interspecific competition restricts a species to its **realised niche** and can drive competitive exclusion.
Intraspecific competition cannot 'exclude' a species from itself — only interspecific competition excludes.

sections.content: The competitive exclusion principle

The competitive exclusion principle states that two species cannot occupy the exact same niche in the same place indefinitely. If two species compete for an identical set of limiting resources, one of them will inevitably be the more effective competitor — even by a small margin — and over time it will secure enough of the shared resource to survive and reproduce while the other declines to local extinction. The principle is often summarised as 'complete competitors cannot coexist'. It was demonstrated in classic experiments in which two closely related species reared together in the same conditions always ended with one persisting and the other dying out, even though each could thrive alone. The principle does not say that similar species can never live together — only that they cannot do so if their niches are truly identical.
\text{Niche}(\text{Species A}) = \text{Niche}(\text{Species B}) \implies \text{coexistence is impossible (one is excluded)}
Resource partitioning and coexistence

The escape from competitive exclusion is for similar species to use the shared resource DIFFERENTLY — resource partitioning. Species may divide a resource in space (feeding in different places, such as insect-eating birds foraging at different heights and on different parts of the same tree), in time (being active at different times of day or in different seasons — temporal partitioning), or by form (taking different sizes or types of the same food, such as seed-eaters specialising on different seed sizes). Partitioning makes the species' realised niches differ, which reduces the intensity of interspecific competition and allows the species to coexist. This is how a community can support many species that would otherwise exclude one another.

items: **Resource partitioning** = using a shared resource in different ways — different places, different times, or different forms/sizes.
Partitioning makes the competing species' **realised niches differ**, so their overlap shrinks.
Reduced overlap means **reduced interspecific competition**, which permits **coexistence**.
It is the mechanism by which competitive exclusion is avoided and species-rich communities are maintained.

sections.content: The niche and community structure

Put together, these ideas explain how a community is organised. Because competitive exclusion forbids identical niches, the species that live together must each occupy a distinct realised niche — they partition the available resources. The community therefore ends up structured by feeding role and resource use: photoautotrophic producers capture energy; consumers, saprotrophs, detritivores and parasites occupy different heterotrophic roles; and even species with broadly similar diets are separated by microhabitat, timing or food size. A species' niche thus determines where it fits in the community and how many similar species can be supported alongside it. The more finely resources are partitioned, the more species a community can hold — so the niche concept links the behaviour of individual species to the diversity and structure of the community as a whole.

sections.question:
Two species of ground finch live on the same island and both eat seeds. Ecologists recorded the percentage of each species' diet made up of seeds of different sizes:

| Seed size (mm) | % of diet, Species A | % of diet, Species B |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| 2 | 45 | 5 |
| 4 | 40 | 10 |
| 6 | 12 | 25 |
| 8 | 3 | 35 |
| 10 | 0 | 25 |

(a) Identify the preferred seed size of each species. [1]
(b) Using the data, explain how the two species are able to coexist despite both eating seeds. [3]
sections.solution:
**(a) Preferred seed size** [1 mark]
Species A prefers small (2 mm) seeds, which make up 45% of its diet; Species B prefers large (8 mm) seeds, which make up 35% of its diet. [1]

**(b) How they coexist** [3 marks]
- The two species partition the seed resource by size — Species A specialises on small seeds (2–4 mm make up 85% of its diet) while Species B specialises on larger seeds (8–10 mm make up 60% of its diet). [1 — resource partitioning by food size]
- Although their diets overlap (both take 6 mm seeds), the overlap is small, so their realised niches differ. [1 — reduced niche overlap]
- This reduces interspecific competition for the most important food sizes, so neither excludes the other and the two species coexist. [1 — reduced competition → coexistence]

*The reasoning must connect data → partitioning → reduced competition → coexistence. Simply stating 'they eat different seeds' without linking it to reduced competition would drop the final mark.*

sections.content: Common mistakes examiners penalise

items: **Treating 'niche' and 'habitat' as synonyms** — the habitat is only the physical address; the niche is the whole role, including feeding and every interaction. State both dimensions.

**Getting fundamental and realised the wrong way round** — the fundamental niche is the LARGER, potential one (no competitors); the realised niche is the SMALLER, actual one. Removing a competitor lets the realised niche EXPAND toward the fundamental.

**Saying saprotrophs and detritivores are the same** — both eat dead matter, but saprotrophs digest EXTERNALLY (secrete enzymes, absorb products) whereas detritivores INGEST detritus and digest it INTERNALLY.
**Blurring autotroph and heterotroph** — an autotroph makes its own organic compounds from CO₂; a heterotroph takes them ready-made. A mixotroph does both. Don't call a decomposer an autotroph.

**Attributing competitive exclusion to intraspecific competition** — exclusion is driven by INTERspecific competition between different species with overlapping niches; intraspecific competition is within one species.
**Describing resource partitioning without the consequence** — you must link it to REDUCED competition and hence COEXISTENCE, not just say the species 'do things differently'.
**Claiming similar species can never coexist** — the exclusion principle forbids IDENTICAL niches only; partitioning lets similar species coexist by making their realised niches differ.

sections.content: Model answer — marked the way our engine marks it

B4.2 explanation questions are marked analytically: each distinct, valid biological point is worth one mark, up to the total available. Method-style points (M) credit correct reasoning, answer points (A) credit a correct conclusion, and error-carried-forward (ECF) means one weak line does not sink the marks that follow, provided the rest of your reasoning stands. The mark scheme accepts equivalent wording, so the key is to make each idea DISTINCT — repeating the same point in two forms scores once. Study how each mark below is tied to a specific named idea.

sections.question: Explain the competitive exclusion principle and how resource partitioning allows similar species to coexist. [4]
sections.solution:
**Model answer.**
The competitive exclusion principle states that two species occupying the same niche — that is, requiring the same limiting resources in the same place — cannot coexist indefinitely. One species will be the more effective competitor for that shared resource and will gradually secure enough of it to survive and reproduce, while the other declines to local extinction; the better competitor excludes the weaker one. Similar species can avoid this outcome through resource partitioning: they use the shared resource differently — in different places, at different times, or in different forms or sizes. This makes their realised niches differ, so the overlap between them shrinks. As a result, interspecific competition is reduced, and the two species are able to coexist in the same community.

---
**How our marking engine awards the 4 marks** — each distinct valid point scores 1 (maximum 4):

- **Point 1 (M):** two species with the same/identical niche (same limiting resources) cannot coexist indefinitely.
- **Point 2 (A):** the more effective competitor excludes the other / drives it to local extinction.
- **Point 3 (M):** resource partitioning = using the shared resource differently — in different places / at different times / in different forms.
- **Point 4 (A):** this reduces (interspecific) competition, so the species coexist — the conclusion of the reasoning chain.

The engine credits DISTINCT ideas and accepts equivalents: 'complete competitors cannot coexist', 'outcompetes', 'partition the resource', or a worked example such as birds feeding at different heights all earn the relevant point. ECF applies — a candidate who states partitioning and coexistence correctly still earns those marks even if the exclusion statement is imprecise. Because the four points are independent, there is no all-or-nothing penalty; each valid line stands on its own.

**What the engine rejects.** 'The species just share the resource' scores nothing for partitioning unless it names HOW they use it differently and links this to REDUCED competition. Likewise 'one species wins' scores only if it is tied to competing for the same limiting resource. Vague statements without the causal link do not earn the analytic marks.

**Bottom line:** to secure all 4 marks, make four distinct points — identical niches can't coexist → better competitor excludes the other → partitioning means using the resource differently → this reduces competition and allows coexistence.

sections.content: The Practice button marks YOUR answer this same way — point by point, accepting equivalent wording, applying ECF, and capping at the marks available. So write in distinct, creditable statements rather than one long sentence: name the identical-niche condition, name the exclusion outcome, define partitioning with an example of using a resource differently, then state that competition is reduced and the species coexist. Each named idea is a mark; a fluent paragraph that blurs them together leaves marks on the table.
Where this leads

The niche concept is the backbone of community ecology. Modes of nutrition set up food chains and food webs and the energy flow through them; competition and partitioning explain species richness and how communities assemble; and the fundamental-versus-realised distinction underlies why species' ranges shift when competitors or climate change. Master the habit of describing a species by its full niche — how it feeds, the conditions it tolerates and how it interacts — and you have a framework that carries straight into the study of populations, energy flow and biodiversity.
